skill.name: security-best-practices
skill.description: Guides AI assistants through web application security hardening, including HTTPS, security headers, input validation, CSRF controls, secret handling, API authentication, and OWASP checks.
skill.summary: Harden web applications with practical security controls and OWASP-aligned review guidance.
skill.author: supercent-io

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported critical combinations, but review found no executable skill code, no prompt injection, and no data exfiltration path. Most flagged items are Markdown, TypeScript, YAML, and bash examples inside SKILL.md. Medium risk remains because some security guidance examples use weaker patterns, including inline CSP allowances and raw refresh-token storage.

medium_findings.title: Security Guidance Allows Inline CSP Sources
medium_findings.description: The CSP example allows inline scripts and styles. This is not malicious, but a security skill should avoid presenting weaker CSP defaults without warning because copied guidance can increase XSS exposure.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines explicitly include unsafe-inline values in a Content Security Policy example. The risk is instructional rather than executable, but the security impact is clear if users copy it.

medium_findings.title: Refresh Tokens Stored Without Hashing Guidance
medium_findings.description: The authentication example stores refresh tokens directly in a database record. It does not explain hashing tokens before storage, which can increase account takeover risk after database exposure.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The example assigns refresh-token values into database fields. Legitimate implementations vary, but secure guidance should usually recommend storing a hash of bearer refresh tokens.

low_findings.title: Static eval Finding Is a Prohibition, Not Execution
low_findings.description: The detected eval text appears in a prohibited-items rule that says not to use eval. No evidence found of eval being called by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is plain Markdown guidance that warns against eval. It is not executable code and contains no user-controlled input flow.

low_findings.title: External Command Detections Are Markdown Delimiters and Examples
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick hits are code fences and inline Markdown tags. No evidence found of runtime shell execution, child process use, or command injection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding context is documentation and fenced sample code. There is no script file or instruction that executes these snippets automatically.

low_findings.title: Network Findings Are Example URLs and References
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs and fetch text appear in CSP examples, an HTTPS redirect example, a commented client request, and reference links. No evidence found of unauthorized outbound network behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation examples or public references. The fetch call is commented in a client usage example and does not collect or transmit secrets.

low_findings.title: Credential Strings Are Placeholders in Secret-Management Examples
low_findings.description: The database URL, token names, and API key names are placeholder examples used to teach secret management. They are not real credentials, but they should remain clearly marked as examples.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The values use obvious placeholders such as local database URLs and xxx suffixes. The same section explicitly instructs users not to commit environment files.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal Findings Are Relative Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The ../ sequences are Markdown links to related skill documents. No evidence found of filesystem reads, writes, or path traversal logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are static Markdown links. They are not used in code paths that access the filesystem.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Scanner Hits Lack Supporting Evidence
low_findings.description: Static analysis reported weak cryptographic algorithm patterns, but the cited lines do not show MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or similar weak algorithms. No evidence found for this issue.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain descriptive metadata, HSTS includeSubDomains, and an OWASP checklist item. None contains a weak cryptographic primitive.

low_findings.title: Critical Heuristic Combination Is Not Confirmed Semantically
low_findings.description: The scanner combined code execution, network, filesystem, and credential indicators, but review found documentation examples only. No evidence found of malicious intent, obfuscation, or exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All cited indicators occur inside SKILL.md documentation. There are no executable helper scripts, encoded payloads, hidden prompts, or network destinations controlled by the skill author.

dangerous_patterns.title: Inline CSP Allowances in Example Code
dangerous_patterns.description: The security-header example includes inline script and style allowances. This is a dangerous pattern when copied into production without nonce, hash, or strict-dynamic alternatives.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The CSP directives are explicit and easy to copy. The danger depends on deployment context, so confidence is high for guidance risk but not for malicious behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Plain Refresh Token Persistence in Example Code
dangerous_patterns.description: The JWT example stores bearer refresh tokens directly. Production guidance should recommend hashing stored refresh tokens and comparing hashes during rotation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The persistence pattern is visible in the example. The exact risk depends on surrounding controls that are not shown in the skill.

content.user_title: Harden Web Apps with Security Best Practices
content.value_statement: Web applications often ship with weak headers, unsafe inputs, and fragile authentication controls. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured security guidance for common hardening tasks.
actual_capabilities: Guides HTTPS enforcement and common security-header setup for Express applications.
Shows input validation, output sanitization, and parameterized-query patterns.
Explains CSRF token setup for state-changing API requests.
Provides examples for environment-based secret management and Kubernetes Secrets.
Outlines JWT access-token and refresh-token rotation concepts.
Includes an OWASP Top 10 checklist for structured application review.
limitations: Examples focus mainly on Node.js and Express applications.
The skill is guidance only and does not run automated vulnerability scans.
Some examples are simplified and need production review before use.
It does not replace a professional penetration test or compliance assessment.

use_cases.title: Secure a new API before launch
use_cases.description: Apply headers, rate limits, input validation, and CSRF controls before exposing endpoints to users.

use_cases.title: Prepare an application security review
use_cases.description: Use the OWASP checklist to organize findings and identify missing controls in an existing service.

use_cases.title: Improve secret handling in deployment
use_cases.description: Move credentials into environment variables or platform secrets and document safer operational patterns.

prompt_templates.title: Review basic API hardening
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner security review for an Express API
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to review my Express API for HTTPS, security headers, rate limiting, input validation, and CSRF gaps. Give prioritized fixes.

prompt_templates.title: Create an OWASP checklist
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to create an OWASP Top 10 review checklist for my web application. Include evidence to collect and likely remediation tasks.

prompt_templates.title: Design secret management controls
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design secret management for my service across local development, CI, and Kubernetes. Include rotation and access-control recommendations.

prompt_templates.title: Audit authentication architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to audit my JWT access-token and refresh-token design. Check token lifetime, rotation, revocation, storage, hashing, and abuse detection.

output_examples.input: Review my public API before launch.
output: A prioritized hardening list covering HTTPS, headers, rate limits, validation, CSRF, and authentication controls.
A short risk explanation for each missing control.
Implementation notes for Express middleware and deployment configuration.

output_examples.input: Help me prepare for an OWASP review.
output: A checklist mapped to OWASP Top 10 categories.
Questions to ask during code review and deployment review.
Suggested evidence, such as configuration files, middleware setup, and authentication flows.

output_examples.input: Improve how my app handles secrets.
output: A plan to move secrets out of source code and into environment or platform secret stores.
Guidance for local development, CI, and Kubernetes environments.
Follow-up checks for rotation, least privilege, and accidental exposure.

best_practices: Use the skill as a checklist, then verify each recommendation against your framework and deployment environment.
Replace placeholder secrets, domains, and token names with secure values from your own secret store.
Pair the guidance with tests, dependency scanning, and manual review for high-risk features.
anti_patterns: Do not copy simplified examples into production without reviewing edge cases and current library guidance.
Do not treat the checklist as proof of compliance or penetration-test coverage.
Do not expose credentials, tokens, or real environment values when asking an AI assistant for help.

faq.question: Does this skill run security scans?
faq.answer: No. It provides review guidance and implementation patterns, but it does not execute scanners or inspect systems automatically.

faq.question: Which platforms does it support?
faq.answer: The report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code. The source metadata also mentions other AI platforms.

faq.question: Is this only for Express applications?
faq.answer: Most code examples use Express, but the security concepts apply to many web application frameworks.

faq.question: Can it replace a security engineer?
faq.answer: No. It helps structure work, but high-risk systems still need expert review and testing.

faq.question: What should users be careful about?
faq.answer: Review examples before copying them, especially CSP settings, token storage, and secret-management details.

faq.question: Does it contain malicious behavior?
faq.answer: No evidence found of malicious behavior. The audit found documentation examples rather than executable exfiltration or command execution.
